Name: adaptive_rate_distortion_modeling
Title: Adaptive Rate-Distortion Framework for Model Compression in Neural Networks
Short Hypothesis: An adaptive rate-distortion framework can significantly enhance model compression by dynamically allocating bits to layers based on their contribution to task-relevant information, outperforming static methods.
Related Work: While existing works, such as 'An Information-Theoretic Justification for Model Pruning' and 'Population Risk Improvement with Model Compression', explore rate-distortion theory and model compression, they often rely on static bit allocation strategies. This proposal distinguishes itself by introducing a dynamic, adaptive framework that continuously analyzes layer contributions, allowing for a more efficient allocation of compression resources.
Abstract: This research aims to develop an adaptive rate-distortion framework for model compression in neural networks, where the bit allocation for each layer is dynamically adjusted based on its task-relevant information contribution. By treating the compression of neural network parameters as a communication channel, we will derive a novel rate-distortion function that reflects the changing importance of different layers throughout the training process. We hypothesize that this adaptive approach will lead to improved performance compared to traditional static compression methods, as it allows the model to maintain essential features while minimizing unnecessary complexity. The proposed framework will be validated through a series of experiments involving standard benchmark datasets, and we will compare the performance of our adaptive method against existing static techniques in terms of both accuracy and compression efficiency.
Experiments: 1. Implement the proposed adaptive rate-distortion framework in a standard neural network architecture (e.g., ResNet or ViT).
2. Conduct experiments on benchmark datasets (e.g., CIFAR-10, ImageNet) to compare the performance of the adaptive method against static compression techniques.
3. Evaluate the model performance using metrics such as accuracy, compression ratio, and task-relevant distortion.
4. Analyze the distribution of bit allocation across layers to illustrate the effectiveness of the adaptive strategy.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The dynamic nature of the proposed framework may introduce additional computational overhead during training.
The effectiveness of the adaptive approach may depend on the specific architecture and task, potentially limiting generalizability.
The development of the rate-distortion function may require extensive theoretical groundwork, which could pose challenges in implementation.
